summary:
The Tiszapolgar culture flourished in the northeastern Carpathian Basin — primarily present-day eastern Hungary and western Romania — from approximately 4500 to 4000 BC, forming part of the broader Chalcolithic transformation of the region. It developed out of earlier Neolithic traditions and is characterized by distinctive pottery forms including tall pedestaled bowls and handled jugs, as well as increased use of copper for tools and ornaments. Burial evidence from cemeteries such as Tiszapolgar-Basatanya reveals growing social differentiation, with some individuals interred with significantly more grave goods than others.

The Tiszapolgar culture participated in the exchange networks of Chalcolithic southeastern Europe, receiving copper from Balkan sources and contributing to the spread of metallurgical practices northward and westward into Central Europe. The culture represents a transitional phase between the earlier Lengyel and Tisza Neolithic cultures and the subsequent Bodrogkeresztur culture, reflecting the dynamic cultural transformations of the fifth millennium BC in this strategically located region. Its cemeteries are important sources for understanding the social organization of early Copper Age communities in the Carpathian zone.
